content:
Your app pod has two memory limits. You only set one.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Cheaper than the hour you’ll lose next time this happens — if you read this first.
$3,800 in failed payments. 90 seconds of egress blackout. Root cause: a 64 MiB sidecar proxy that needed 71 MiB during a config reload burst.
The main container was healthy. Liveness passed. Readiness passed. CPU 22%. Memory 41% of its 2 GiB limit.
Every outbound call — Stripe, fraud check, email — timed out. Because the Envoy sidecar sharing the pod network namespace was OOMKilled, and without it, nothing left the pod.
Istio intercepts outbound traffic with iptables REDIRECT rules. App thinks it connects to Stripe on port 443. Traffic goes to localhost Envoy on 15001. Envoy dies — connection hangs or resets. App logs say “network timeout.” Network is fine. Proxy is dead.
Five things to check when egress dies but the app looks fine
1. Check all containers in the pod — not just the app
kubectl get pod POD -o jsonpath='{range .status.containerStatuses[*]}{.name}{": "}{.state}{"\n"}{end}'
kubectl get pod POD -o jsonpath='{range .status.initContainerStatuses[*]}{.name}{": "}{.state}{"\n"}{end}'Look for OOMKilled on istio-proxy, linkerd-proxy, vault-agent, or custom sidecars.
2. Compare memory limit vs actual peak
kubectl top pod POD --containers
# app: 840Mi / 2Gi
# istio-proxy: 63Mi / 64Mi ← one megabyte from death3. Check restart count on sidecar only
kubectl describe pod POD | grep -A3 "istio-proxy"
# Last State: Terminated, Reason: OOMKilled, Exit Code: 1374. Test egress from app container
kubectl exec POD -c app -- curl -sS -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" --max-time 5 https://api.stripe.com
# 000 or timeout — egress path broken5. Egress failure runbook step one — ten seconds
kubectl get pod POD -o jsonpath='{range .status.containerStatuses[*]}{.name}{" "}{.lastState.terminated.reason}{"\n"}{end}'Tells you if proxy OOMed before you tcpdump.
Why sidecar OOM is invisible to app monitoring
Your app dashboards track the app container. Prometheus container_memory_working_set_bytes filtered by container="app" stays flat.
Sidecar spikes during:
→ Config hot reload — xDS push, route table rebuild → TLS certificate rotation — memory spike parsing new certs → Traffic burst — connection table growth → WASM filter load — someone added a custom filter in staging, promoted to prod
The sidecar dies. Kubernetes restarts only the sidecar container or the whole pod. During restart window — 30 to 90 seconds with our settings — outbound traffic fails.
Ingress still worked. Health checks hit the app directly. Load balancer kept sending traffic to a pod that could receive but not pay.
The memory math nobody does
Pod memory limit in Kubernetes is per container, not per pod. But the node schedules based on sum of container limits.
app: 2048 MiB limit
istio-proxy: 64 MiB limit ← set by platform team in 2021
vault-agent: 32 MiB limit
─────────────────────────────
Total: 2144 MiB scheduledIstio proxy memory during config reload: 71 MiB. OOMKilled. App untouched.
Fix was not raising app memory. Fix was:
resources:
requests:
memory: 128Mi
limits:
memory: 256Mi # was 64Mi$3,800 outage. 192 MiB of headroom would have prevented it.
Platform teams set conservative sidecar limits years ago when route tables were small. Microservices grew. VirtualService count hit 400. xDS snapshot size grew 8×. The limit stayed at 64 MiB because nobody re-profiled.
Seven sidecar OOM patterns we have seen

• Default proxy limit too low — platform template from 2019, never updated

• Config dump growth — thousands of routes from unbounded virtual services

• WASM filters — memory hog loaded at runtime

• mTLS cert churn — short-lived certs, frequent reload

• Connection pool explosion — app opens 5000 connections, proxy tracks all

• HPA on app only — more app replicas, same sidecar limit, more proxy memory globally

• Co-located logging sidecar — Fluent Bit + mesh proxy competing for cgroup on small pods
Diagnosis commands — copy-paste
# All container states in namespace
kubectl get pods -n prod -o custom-columns=\
POD:.metadata.name,\
APP:.status.containerStatuses[0].restartCount,\
PROXY:.status.containerStatuses[1].restartCount,\
PROXY_REASON:.status.containerStatuses[1].lastState.terminated.reason# Memory per container last hour (Prometheus)
sum by (container) (
container_memory_working_set_bytes{pod="POD", namespace="prod"}
)# Recent OOM events
kubectl get events -n prod --field-selector reason=OOMKilling | tail -20
dmesg -T | grep -i oom | tail -10Linkerd and Envoy — same problem, different binary
Linkerd proxy OOM looks identical in symptoms. Main container healthy, egress dead, linkerd-proxy at memory limit. Diagnosis commands change — linkerd diagnostics instead of istioctl proxy-config — but the pattern is the same: check every container in the pod.
Ambassador, NGINX sidecar, Consul connect — all share the pod network namespace. All can OOM independently. All can kill egress while ingress health checks pass.
Platform dashboard now shows max memory % per container in a pod, not pod aggregate. Aggregate hid our proxy at 99% while app sat at 41%.
Fixes that stick

• Right-size sidecar limits — profile under load, add 40% headroom

• Alert on sidecar restarts — kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{container=~".*proxy.*"}

• Alert on sidecar memory — 80% of limit, not OOM

• Config pruning — reduce xDS payload, split services

• Native sidecar containers (Kubernetes 1.28+) — proper lifecycle ordering; still need memory

• Egress fallback review — document what happens when proxy dies
We updated the platform Helm chart: proxy limit 256 MiB default, alert at 200 MiB, dashboard row for proxy memory per pod.
Istio proxyMetadata can tune proxy memory in some versions. Read the mesh version docs before blindly doubling limits. Sometimes the fix is fewer VirtualServices, not more MiB. We deleted 120 stale routes nobody owned. Proxy memory dropped 40% without raising limits.
Liveness probe on the app container does not check sidecar health. Add a composite readiness check or monitor sidecar restarts independently. Kubernetes considers the pod ready when all containers pass readiness — but if your readiness only hits the app port, sidecar can be dead while traffic flows.
Payment pods now have annotation egress-critical: "true" in our monitoring. Any sidecar restart on those pods pages immediately — not batched into hourly digest. 90 seconds of dead egress cost $3,800. Immediate page is cheaper.
Sidecar resource requests should be non-zero. We ran proxies at 0 requests and 64Mi limit — Kubernetes scheduled too many pods per node, proxies competed for memory, OOM frequency increased. Requests match limits now for mesh containers. Fewer pods per node, healthier proxies.
The invoice

• 90 seconds egress down

• ~1,200 payment attempts failed

• Average order $3.17

• $3,800 direct loss

• 4 hours engineering investigation because we looked at the app first
Cheaper than the hour you’ll lose if you only kubectl top the main container.
→ Docker War Room — OOMKilled diagnosis tree, CrashLoopBackOff patterns, container memory emergencies, and the 15 fix patterns for when Docker and Kubernetes break in production. Five-minute first response.
Run kubectl top pod --containers during peak traffic monthly. Screenshot proxy memory trend. Attach to capacity review. Invisible sidecars become visible when you schedule looking at them.
Green app container plus dead proxy equals red revenue. Monitor both or own the invoice. kubectl describe pod shows OOMKilled on sidecar in Last State. Read the whole describe output. Not just the app container section.
We added egress smoke test to deploy pipeline — curl Stripe from new pod before marking deploy healthy. Catches dead proxy in 15 seconds instead of 90 seconds of failed payments.
Platform SREs now include sidecar memory in capacity planning spreadsheets. App memory + proxy memory + logging sidecar memory = pod budget. Not app memory alone.
Mesh upgrades that restart all sidecars simultaneously recreate egress risk even without application deploy. Coordinate mesh rollouts with maxUnavailable: 1 on both app and mesh dataplane.
Readiness probe that only checks app port is a lie when Istio owns egress. Fix the probe or accept the invoice.
64 MiB was enough in 2021. It is not enough with 400 VirtualServices. Profile again.
Next time egress dies and the app looks fine, check the sidecar first. Not last. Not never.
Four hours of debugging app timeouts. OOMKilled proxy in describe output the whole time. We just were not looking at the second container.
Cheaper than $3,800 in ninety seconds. Profile the sidecar. Check every container.
Your app is not one container. It is every container in the pod. Monitor all of them or pay the difference.
Next outage drill: Froquiz runs 10,000+ scenario questions on Docker, Kubernetes, and production debugging — Senior Dev Challenges that ask what you’d check first when the app is green and customers are angry.
→ Froquiz
